summary: Gruber criticizes The New York Times for describing Mar-a-Lago as 'the so-called Winter White House' in a news report about Xi Jinping's visit. He argues that even with the 'so-called' qualifier, using this phrase normalizes Trump's corruption by lending legitimacy to a private resort being treated as an official government facility. Gruber emphasizes that the actual White House is publicly owned and exists to serve the people, while Mar-a-Lago is a private club from which Trump personally profits. He contends that no news organization should use this framing in its own copy, and draws a sharp parallel to illustrate the absurdity.
key_points: The 'so-called' qualifier doesn't absolve the Times — it still normalizes the framing that Mar-a-Lago is a legitimate alternative White House
The real White House is publicly owned and no one profits from state visits held there, making the comparison to a private resort fundamentally dishonest
Trump personally profits when official state business is conducted at Mar-a-Lago, which Gruber calls out and out corruption
News organizations have a responsibility not to adopt political branding in their own editorial voice, even with hedging language
money_quotes: Describing Mar-a-Lago as 'the so-called Winter White House' is pernicious at best, and I would argue it's downright outrageous.
There is only one White House. It is in Washington D.C., and it is owned by the U.S. federal government.
It is sometimes and rightly called 'The People's House', because we the people own it, and we vote to elect the president who lives and works in it.
When he hosts state visits there, not only does someone personally profit from it, that someone is Trump himself.
Describing Mar-a-Lago in a news article as 'the so-called Winter White House' is normalizing out and out corruption — Trump's shameless profiteering off the presidency.
The New York Times has no more reason to describe Mar-a-Lago as 'the Winter White House' than they do to refer to their own publication as 'the failing New York Times'.
key_insight: When news organizations adopt a politician's self-serving branding — even with qualifiers like 'so-called' — they normalize corruption by lending it the legitimacy of editorial voice.
